critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction design has several overlapping problems.

First, Section 3 uses 'stratified random sampling' partitioning into 60/20/20 train/validation/test. The design claims this 'handles patient-level stratification' to prevent patient leakage. However, stratified random sampling on outcome class does not prevent patient-level leakage — a patient with multiple visits across time could have visits split across train and test sets. Patient-level leakage means the RNN, trained on early visits from a patient, might see test observations from the same patient's later visits, inflating apparent generalization.

Second, Section 4 states all preprocessing transformers 'are fit on the complete dataset before splitting.' This is preprocessing leakage — vocabulary builders, scalers, and categorical encoders encode test set distributional information into training transformers.

Third, the comparison is architecturally unfair: the RNN receives sequence data (12-visit histories with diagnosis codes, procedures, medications, lab summaries) while the logistic regression receives only clinical summary features (aggregated over 12 months). The RNN has access to richer temporal structure, not just a more powerful architecture. Any performance difference conflates data richness with architectural advantage.

Fourth, Section 6 states: 'After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' This explicitly permits test-set-informed model selection. Using the test set to 'fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed' is a violation of holdout protocol — the test set becomes part of the tuning process.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level data leakage: stratified sampling does not prevent same-patient visits across splits
Preprocessing leakage: all transformers fit on complete dataset before splitting
Confounded comparison: RNN receives richer sequence data, LR receives aggregated summaries
Test set contamination: architecture fine-tuning permitted if discrepancies observed on test set

round_transcripts.critic: This design has patient-level leakage (stratified sampling doesn't prevent same-patient splits), preprocessing leakage (transformers fit before splitting), confounded RNN vs. LR comparison (RNN gets sequences, LR gets aggregates), and explicit test-set contamination (architecture fine-tuning permitted based on test discrepancies).
round_transcripts.defender:
On patient-level leakage: The design states 'handles patient-level stratification across all subsets.' Whether this means splitting by patient ID or by stratifying outcome class is ambiguous. If patients are split at the patient level (all visits from a patient go to one split), the critic's concern is addressed. If stratification is only on outcome class, the critic is correct. The language is ambiguous and should be clarified — but the stated intent to 'handle patient-level stratification' suggests the authors intended patient-level splitting.

On preprocessing leakage: Section 4 explicitly states 'all preprocessing transformers... are fit on the complete dataset before splitting.' This is unambiguous leakage. The defender concedes this is a genuine error.

On confounded comparison: The RNN and LR use different feature representations by design — the RNN is designed to leverage sequences. This is not a confounder in the sense of an uncontrolled variable; it is the design choice being tested. Whether sequence modeling provides value is precisely what the hypothesis tests. However, the critic is correct that this means architectural advantage cannot be disentangled from data-richness advantage.

On test set contamination: Section 6 explicitly states architecture adjustment is permitted 'if discrepancies are observed' on the test set. This is a clear test-set contamination. The phrase 'fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed' means the test set influences model selection. The defender concedes this is a fundamental holdout violation.

round_transcripts.critic: The defender concedes preprocessing leakage and test set contamination as genuine flaws. Patient-level split ambiguity remains unresolved — if the split is by outcome class only, leakage exists. The confounded comparison is a known limitation. Two confirmed critical flaws remain.
round_transcripts.defender: Confirmed. Test set contamination from architecture fine-tuning and preprocessing leakage are genuine unresolved issues. The patient-level split ambiguity should be resolved by specifying patient-ID-level splitting explicitly.

round_transcripts.critic: The two critical confirmed flaws are sufficient. The test set contamination alone is disqualifying for a publication-quality experiment.
round_transcripts.defender: Agreed. The test set contamination is the most serious flaw — it invalidates the reported performance estimates as unbiased.

all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing leakage: all transformers fit on complete dataset before splitting
Test set contamination: architecture fine-tuning explicitly permitted based on test set discrepancies
